_meta.description: Keyword → stain family mapping for family-level fallback matching

protein.chemistry: Protein molecules denature and coagulate. Heat sets them permanently. Cold water only for fresh stains.
protein.treatment_principle: Cold water only. Never heat. Enzyme digestion (except silk/wool). NSD for delicates. Protein denaturation is irreversible above 140°F.
contraindications: Protease enzymes on silk (digests fiber protein)

tannin.chemistry: Tannins are polyphenolic compounds that form hydrogen bonds with cellulose and protein fibers. Acidic conditions and oxidation can help release them.
tannin.treatment_principle: Tannin formula (acetic acid or citric acid). Warm water OK after initial treatment. Never bleach first (can oxidize and set). Cold rinse initially, then warm treatment.
contraindications: Chlorine bleach first (oxidizes tannin to darker color)

oil.chemistry: Lipids and non-polar hydrocarbons don't dissolve in water. Require surfactants (POG) or solvents to emulsify and lift. Heat helps emulsification.
oil.treatment_principle: POG (Petroleum, Oil, Grease) removal or surfactant lift. Warm water helps emulsify. Pre-treat before wash. Pat dry after initial treatment, don't rub.
contraindications: Chlorine bleach on some oils (may set)

dye.chemistry: Chromophoric molecules (colored dyes) bond to fibers through hydrogen bonds or Van der Waals forces. Require solvent extraction or chromophore breaking via oxidizer/reducer.
dye.treatment_principle: Solvent for carrier, then target chromophore. Test colorfast first. Oxidizer (H2O2) or reducer (sodium hydrosulfite) depending on dye type. Never apply oxidizer + reducer together.
contraindications: Bleach on natural dyes without testing (may discolor fabric)
recommended_agents: Acetone (for some synthetic dyes — NOT silk/wool)

oxidizable.chemistry: Oxidized metal ions (Fe3+, Cu2+) form colored compounds. Require reducing agents to convert back to soluble form, or chelation to bind metal ion.
oxidizable.treatment_principle: Reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) to break oxidized bonds. Oxalic acid for rust. Never chlorine bleach on rust (oxidizes further, darkens stain). Oxalic acid + heat most effective.
contraindications: Oxalic acid on certain metals (may damage)

combo_protein_tannin.chemistry: Contains both protein (milk/egg) and tannin (coffee/chocolate/cocoa). Protein will set permanently if exposed to heat before tannin removal. Must treat protein-first to avoid setting.
combo_protein_tannin.treatment_principle: PROTEIN FIRST (cold enzyme or NSD), THEN tannin. Never reverse order. Cold rinse → enzyme (if fiber safe) → acid rinse → oxidizer if needed. Each step must complete before next.
contraindications: Skipping enzyme step on washable fabrics (protein remains)

combo_oil_dye.chemistry: Oil or wax carries pigment/dye. Pigment alone is insoluble in water; must remove carrier first to expose dye, then target dye separately.
combo_oil_dye.treatment_principle: Remove oil/wax carrier first (POG/solvent), then target dye component. Solvent lifts carrier, then acid/oxidizer attacks dye. Two-phase treatment required.
recommended_agents: Step 1: Rubbing alcohol or acetone (remove carrier — test first)

resin.chemistry: Polymeric resins and adhesives bond mechanically to fibers. Solvents dissolve polymer chains; freezing embrittles for mechanical removal. Chemistry varies by resin type.
resin.treatment_principle: Physical removal first (scrape/freeze). Then solvent (acetone, amyl acetate) — CHECK FIBER SAFETY. Freeze for 30min → pick off → solvent as last resort. Never melt adhesive with heat.
recommended_agents: Peanut butter or mayonnaise (on some gums)
Amyl acetate (safer than acetone on some fibers)

particulate.chemistry: Insoluble particles suspended or dry on fiber surface. Require mechanical removal (dry brushing) first, then targeted wash for any remaining chemical residue. Wet treatment before dry may set particles deeper.
particulate.treatment_principle: Let dry, brush off particulate first. Then treat any remaining chemical stain. Dry brush with soft brush → tap gently → vacuum → then wet treatment if needed. Hard water particles may need acidic rinse.
contraindications: Hot water on mineral deposits (may require acid)
recommended_agents: Soft brush or lint roller (mechanical removal)

mildew.chemistry: Living organism (fungal spores) and their metabolic byproducts. Smoke/soot are carbon particulates. Require killing organism first (oxidizer), then removing stain/odor. Soot requires special dry-clean solvent.
mildew.treatment_principle: Kill organism (oxidizer), then remove stain. Spore safety critical for mildew. Smoke/soot best handled by professional dry cleaning. Vinegar + peroxide for mildew. Sunlight + air for odor.
mildew.safety_note: Mildew spores can trigger respiratory reactions. Use mask if cleaning. Soot is carbon particulate — may require professional cleaning.
contraindications: Chlorine bleach on colored fabrics (may discolor)

chemical_damage.chemistry: Not a stain — fiber or dye has been chemically altered or destroyed. Bleach removes chromophores (lightens). Acid can hydrolyze cellulose. Alkaline can denature protein fibers. Damage is irreversible.
chemical_damage.treatment_principle: Not stain removal — damage assessment. Dye restoration (fabric paint) or re-dyeing options. Document damage, stop treatment. Call professional for restoration estimate. No cleaning method reverses this.
contraindications: Any stain removal method (won't help, may worsen)
recommended_action: File claim with cleaner if professional caused damage

unknown.chemistry: Unknown stain requires diagnostic testing to classify into one of the above families. Start with safest assumptions (cold water, gentle) and test response to guide next steps.
unknown.treatment_principle: Diagnostic sequence: test with cold water, NSD, then targeted agents based on response. If brown/red + warm water sets it → protein. If lightens with acid → tannin. If wax carrier visible → resin or oil-dye.
diagnostic_sequence: Inspect under good light (color, texture, shine)
Neutral detergent solution test (does it lift?)
Test with vinegar (acidity test — does color change?)
Test with rubbing alcohol (solvent test — does stain move?)
If protein suspected: enzyme on hidden area (delicate test)
